texto: You tell me to stand still, but I am not walking," he shouted, "whereas you who are walking say you are still. How is it that you are standing still but I am not?"The Buddha turned round. "My legs move but my mind is still," he said. "Your legs are still but your mind moves all the time in a fire of anger, hatred, and feverish desire. Therefore, I am still but you are not.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Majjhimanikaya que provoca passagem do tempo e prioridades. Ao questionar “You tell me to stand still, but I am not walking," he shouted, "whereas you who are walking say y…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre reflexao, buddhism, still, com foco em reflexao.

frase_original: You tell me to stand still, but I am not walking," he shouted, "whereas you who are walking say you are still. How is it that you are standing still but I am not?"The Buddha turned round. "My legs move but my mind is still," he said. "Your legs are still but your mind moves all the time in a fire of anger, hatred, and feverish desire. Therefore, I am still but you are not.
